content.text:
That's a much better architecture than what I was advocating, and I want to walk through why before agreeing too quickly.

## Why this is the right shape

The github.io blob-URL detour I was pushing trades aesthetics for traceability — you'd lose the curated landing page, the brand-clean URL, the dark-mode styling, and accept "staging" in every link. Per-version subdirectories under github.io let you have *all* of it:

- Brand-clean URL: `https://scaleinv.github.io/recipebottle/v1.2.3#Payor`
- Curated presentation: each version's `index.html` is a real rendered page (pandoc, faux-render, whatever)
- Pinned to binary version: customer running v1.2.3 only ever sees v1.2.3 docs
- Iterable: dev runs point at a "latest" or "current" or "main" subdirectory you control
- Immutable history: old versions stay published forever; you can re-link a customer to docs that match their binary even if you've shipped twelve releases since

The killer property is that **`index.html` doesn't have to die — it just has to live in a version-namespaced directory.** The "obsoleting" was solving the wrong problem; the actual problem was that `index.html` lived at the root with no version dimension. Adding a directory per version is the smallest change that fixes the actual problem.

This also reverses the trade-off on the consumer template wrinkle. Now `index.html` is no longer "the crufty cosmology page" — it's "the current version's rendered README." Which means the publishing pipeline starts to look like: **README.md (source) → pandoc → versioned `index.html` → github.io subdirectory.** The cosmology page becomes a separate concern (or gets folded in as a section of the README, or gets retired).

## The shape of the regime variable

You're right that RBRR is the natural home — it's repo-level config that varies per release. The question is what shape to give it. Two coherent options:

**Option α — Whole URL in RBRR:**
```bash
# In rbrr.env (regime instance, set at release time)
RBRR_PUBLIC_DOCS_URL="https://scaleinv.github.io/recipebottle/v1.2.3"

# In RBGC (constants — gone, replaced by regime read)
# The bug_tlt callers just reference RBRR_PUBLIC_DOCS_URL directly
```

*Pro*: Maximum flexibility. Can point at staging (`https://scaleinv.github.io/recipebottle/staging`), at a feature branch preview directory, at the public versioned directory, at `localhost:8080` for offline preview — anywhere. Dev environments override via `.env` without code changes.

*Con*: No structural enforcement. Nothing prevents someone setting it to gibberish, an http://, an unrelated domain. Validator can check it's `https://`-prefixed and reachable, but format consistency relies on convention.

**Option β — Base + version split:**
```bash
# In RBGC (compile-time constant)
readonly RBGC_PUBLIC_DOCS_URL_BASE="https://scaleinv.github.io/recipebottle"

# In rbrr.env (regime — just the version segment)
RBRR_RELEASE_VERSION="v1.2.3"

# Composed at kindle time somewhere
readonly RBGC_PUBLIC_DOCS_URL="${RBGC_PUBLIC_DOCS_URL_BASE}/${RBRR_RELEASE_VERSION}"
```

*Pro*: Structural enforcement — the base URL is locked, only the version segment is per-release. Validator can check `RBRR_RELEASE_VERSION` matches a tag pattern (`v[0-9]+\.[0-9]+\.[0-9]+` or similar). Harder to accidentally point customers at the wrong domain.

*Con*: Can't easily preview staging or local renders without temporarily editing RBGC. Also commits Recipe Bottle to a particular hosting structure (always `<base>/<version>`) — if you ever want to host on a CDN with a different URL shape, you have to refactor in two places.

## My take

**I'd pick Option α (whole URL in RBRR), but with a stricter validator than the usual RBRR string field.** The validator should:

1. Require `https://` prefix
2. Reject trailing slashes (so the constants generator can append `#Payor` cleanly)
3. Optionally warn if not under `scaleinv.github.io/recipebottle/` — soft enforcement that the convention exists, but still permits override for legitimate alternatives (local preview server, staging fork experimentation)

The flexibility wins because **the iteration loop you want is exactly where Option α earns its keep** — dev runs can point at a `current/` subdirectory, or at staging, or at a local pandoc render in `file:///`, all by editing one `.env` field. Option β makes that harder for no real safety gain.

## What `prep-release` would do

The ceremony grows one step:

> **Step N: Pin docs URL to release version.** Detect the release tag/version being cut. Compute the docs URL: `${RBGC_BASE}/${tag}`. Sed-replace the `RBRR_PUBLIC_DOCS_URL` line in the candidate branch's `rbrr.env`. Commit. (Or, if Option β: sed-replace `RBRR_RELEASE_VERSION` with the bare version.)

And the rendering side grows one step:

> **Step M: Render README.md → versioned `index.html`.** Run pandoc against the candidate branch's `README.md` with the dark-mode CSS shim, output to `index.html`. Stage it for the github.io publishing PR (separate from the candidate branch — it goes to whatever repo serves `scaleinv.github.io/recipebottle/`).

The two new steps are independent: the URL pin happens in the candidate branch (so the binary embeds the right URL), and the rendering happens against the github.io serving repo at a `v{tag}/` directory.

## What the github.io serving repo looks like

```
.nojekyll
LICENSE
podman-gateway-proposal.md
rbm-abstract-drawio.svg
v1.2.3/
  index.html       ← rendered README.md for v1.2.3
v1.2.4/
  index.html
v2.0.0/
  index.html
latest/            ← optional: symlink or duplicate of newest version
  index.html
current/           ← optional: dev rolling preview, points at main
  index.html
index.html         ← root: small page that lists versions or redirects to latest
```

The root `index.html` becomes a router/index of versions. A customer who somehow lands at `scaleinv.github.io/recipebottle/` (without their binary's URL) sees a list of versions or gets bounced to latest. Customers who arrive via `bug_tlt` always have the full URL so they hit their version directly.

## Open questions

A handful of decisions before this can move:

1. **Option α or β?** I lean α; you?

2. **Where does the version string actually come from?** Git tag in this repo? Git tag in the public `scaleinv/recipebottle` repo? Manually-set in rbrr.env? Computed from build provenance? My instinct: a git tag in this dev repo, materialized into RBRR by the prep-release ceremony. Tag this repo `v1.2.3`, prep-release reads it, writes it to the candidate's RBRR.

3. **What's the dev default for `RBRR_PUBLIC_DOCS_URL`?** When you're running from this repo's source tree without having released anything, the URL needs to point somewhere. Three plausible defaults:
   - `https://scaleinv.github.io/recipebottle/current` (dev rolling preview, requires you publish a `current/` directory yourself)
   - `https://scaleinv.github.io/recipebottle/latest` (always-newest, may not match dev source)
   - `file:///path/to/local/render` (offline preview from a `pandoc` invocation)
   - Empty / unset, with a warning, so devs see "no URL configured" and know they're on dev

4. **What about the cosmology page?** It's currently the content of `index.html`. Under the new model, each version's `index.html` is a rendered README — there's no longer a dedicated cosmology page at the root. The cosmology either folds into README.md as a section, gets retired, or moves to its own versioned slot (`v1.2.3/cosmology.html`).

5. **Should we keep the `index.html` faux-rendering tooling that AUAAP built**, or replace it with pandoc-against-README? AUAAP's tooling was sonnet-based MCM attribute resolution against an AsciiDoc spec — that's a different pipeline than markdown→HTML. Probably both pipelines coexist for a while: cosmology stays AsciiDoc-rendered if it survives, README is pandoc-rendered for the per-version pages.

6. **Where does the `index.html` rendering actually happen** — in this repo (where README lives) or in the github.io serving repo (where it's published)? I'd say: render in this repo, commit nothing, the prep-release ceremony picks up the rendered output and ships it. Keeps the dev repo clean of generated artifacts.

I'm not going to try to answer all six in this message — they cluster into "model" (1, 3) and "tooling" (2, 4, 5, 6), and the model questions should land first.

Want to take 1 and 3 next, or is there a different thread you'd rather pull on?

message.content: <local-command-caveat>Caveat: The messages below were generated by the user while running local commands. DO NOT respond to these messages or otherwise consider them in your response unless the user explicitly asks you to.</local-command-caveat>
Unknown skill: buddy

I'm still waffling terribly about what is simplest to do in this situation.  Know that RBS0 is the master set of definitions for all terms, and it even has a 'getting started' gui8de that I think was inspired by our old index.html previous work.  Know that I will _never_ release the .adoc: files: I consider that highly proprietary, our secret specification language.  But maintaining sync between all of these docs is going to be a headache.  I am drawn to the possibilities of pandoc to render html, if for nothing else than enabling SVG links in the picture.  That should work, right?  Help me think through an MVP approach.  Should we conceive of a slash command that directs you how to go from RBS0 subdoc to sync to latest index.html?

content.text: Let me work through what you've put on the table, because the RBS0 constraint is the load-bearing piece I didn't have before. ## Pandoc and SVG: yes, with one caveat Pandoc renders README.md → HTML cleanly, and the SVG-with-links concern resolves like this: - **If you want clickable links *inside* the SVG to jump to glossary anchors in the same HTML page**, the SVG must be **inlined into the HTML** (not referenced via ``). When SVG is embedded as ``, browsers treat it as an opaque image — the `` elements inside don't become hover targets. When it's inlined, the SVG becomes part of the DOM and its links are first-class. - Pandoc can do this in a couple of ways: a Lua filter that reads the .svg and substitutes its contents, or a post-processing step that replaces `` with `$(cat rbm-abstract-drawio.svg)`. Either is ~10 lines of glue. - The .svg itself needs to actually have `` elements wrapping the boxes. drawio supports this on export — you draw the box, set its hyperlink to `#Sentry`, export as SVG, and the resulting file contains the link. So the workflow is: edit in drawio, set link annotations, re-export. No manual SVG editing. So yes, pandoc + inline SVG + drawio's link-on-export gives you a clickable architecture diagram pointing at glossary entries on the same page. That's a real feature — the kind of thing that justifies a custom render pipeline over GitHub's default rendering. GitHub's rendering can't do this; it strips link semantics out of inlined SVG for security. This is the strongest argument I've heard for keeping a render pipeline at all. ## The RBS0 constraint reframes the whole problem Now that I know RBS0 is the proprietary source-of-truth and will never ship, the architecture isn't really "README.md → publish" — it's: ``` RBS0 (proprietary, source of truth) ↓ [sync step — the new problem] README.md (consumer-shaped, can ship) ↓ [pandoc — already solved tool-wise] index.html (ships, with inline SVG, glossary anchors, dark theme) ↓ [versioned publishing — defer] github.io ``` The middle two arrows are tractable. The top arrow is where the headache lives, and where the slash command idea earns its keep. ## Why a slash command is the right shape for the sync step The RBS0 → README.md transformation has properties that make it a poor fit for a script and a good fit for an LLM-assisted command: - **It's not a 1:1 mechanical mapping.** RBS0's glossary entries have technical depth (anchors, mapping section attributes, MCM voicings, internal cross-references) that don't all belong in a consumer README. Each entry has to be re-shaped for a consumer audience while preserving the meaning. That's judgment work, not text substitution. - **It's section-by-section.** A getting-started subsection in RBS0 maps to README's Setup section. The glossary maps to README's Key Concepts table. The cosmology bits map to a narrative paragraph. The mapping is structured but not regular. - **Drift is the normal state.** You edit RBS0 (because that's where definitions live). Now README is stale. The sync isn't a one-shot rewrite — it's a recurring maintenance task. The slash command's main job is to make recurrence cheap. - **Audit beats automation.** What you really want isn't "rewrite README every time RBS0 changes" — it's "show me where they've drifted, and let me approve fixes section by section." That's an interactive ceremony, not a build step. So the slash command shape I'd propose is: > `/rbk-sync-readme` — Compares RBS0 (or a named subdoc, e.g., RBSGS getting-started) against README.md section by section, identifies drift, proposes updates one section at a time, gets user approval before applying each one. Like `cma-prep-pr` or `cma-validate` in spirit — an interactive maintenance ceremony, not an automated transform. It's a sibling to the existing CMA family (cma-normalize, cma-validate, cma-render) — same shape, different domain. ## MVP — what I'd actually propose to build first The MVP collapses to **two things**, and explicitly defers everything else: **MVP-1: The rendering pipeline.** - A small script `Tools/rbk/rbgrh_render_help.sh` (or similar — name TBD) that runs pandoc on `README.md` with: - Dark-theme CSS shim (matching the current `index.html` styling so users see continuity) - Inline SVG substitution for `rbm-abstract-drawio.svg` - Output to a scratch path (`../temp-rbk/help-render/index.html`), not committed - A tabtarget `tt/rbw-gh.RenderHelp.sh` (or whatever fits the colophon tree) that invokes it - A tabtarget `tt/rbw-gho.OpenHelp.sh` that renders + opens in browser for preview This gives you a one-command preview loop. Edit README.md, run the tabtarget, see the rendered page. No publishing yet — the file lives in the scratch directory. **MVP-2: The sync command.** - `/rbk-sync-readme` slash command, shaped like a CMA family member - First version: takes a specific RBS0 subdoc as input (e.g., `RBSGS-GettingStarted.adoc`), shows diffs against the corresponding README section, proposes per-section updates - LLM-driven, interactive, no auto-commit These two together let you iterate: edit RBS0 → run sync command → review proposed README changes → apply → render → preview. All local. No remote pushes, no versioning, no RBRR variables, no prep-release changes. **Explicitly deferred from MVP**: - Per-version subdirectory publishing on github.io - RBRR variable for the docs URL - prep-release ceremony integration - Changing `RBGC_PUBLIC_DOCS_URL` at all - Retiring `README.consumer.md` legacy template The deferred items only become valuable once the rendering pipeline is proven. Today's broken state is "the served page is crufty and links don't land on anything useful." Today's MVP fix is "we have a render pipeline that produces a good local page." The publishing-and-versioning question is the next layer up and shouldn't block the first layer. ## Two questions I want your read on before I'd be comfortable proposing more 1. **Does README.md edit-direction-of-truth matter to you?** Two models: - **RBS0 always leads**: Edit RBS0 first, sync command propagates to README, never edit README directly. One source of truth, slower iteration. - **README leads for consumer-facing changes**: Edit README directly when iterating on the customer experience. Sync command runs in either direction (or just audits drift). Faster iteration, but you have to be disciplined about which file is "right" when they disagree. These imply different slash-command behaviors. RBS0-leads is simpler (the command is one-directional). README-leads is more flexible but the command becomes a diff-and-merge tool. 2. **Does the inline-SVG-with-links property feel important enough to commit to a custom render pipeline forever?** If yes, the GitHub-default-rendering path is permanently off the table — you'll always need pandoc (or equivalent) in the pipeline. That's fine, but it's a commitment. If you'd be OK losing the SVG link feature in exchange for a simpler pipeline, GitHub default rendering becomes viable again as a fallback. My read: the SVG links are worth keeping, but I want to hear yours. If you want, I can also peek at RBS0 (specifically the "getting started guide" section you mentioned) to see how its current shape compares to README's Setup section — that'd give us a concrete sense of how big the sync headache actually is for one section. Just a 10-minute exploration, no edits. Want me to?

content.content:
1	= Getting Started with Recipe Bottle
2	
3	{at_recipe_bottle}
4	constructs container images under controlled conditions on trusted
5	{gcp_service}
6	infrastructure.
7	Developers retrieve these
8	{at_bottle_image_s}
9	and work within identical environments.
10	The
11	{rbtgi_repository}
12	serves as the single source of truth for container artifacts.
13	
14	This guide explains the setup sequence and ongoing operational patterns.
15	Most of what follows happens once; the final sections describe day-to-day work.
16	
17	== Core Concepts
18	
19	{at_recipe_bottle} contains workloads safely.
20	A _vessel_ defines what to build--a specification for a container workload.
21	An {rbtga_ark} is the built result: the immutable artifact produced from that vessel.
22	Multiple {rbtga_ark_s} can exist for a single vessel, each with its own build timestamp and provenance.
23	
24	Each {rbtga_ark} carries strong pedigree information including SBOMs and build metadata.
25	Organizations verify these attestations before deploying.
26	
27	== Depots and Roles
28	
29	A
30	{rbtgi_depot}
31	is the logical facility where container images are constructed and retained.
32	The
33	{rbtgi_repository}
34	within the {rbtgi_depot} stores all
35	{rbtgi_image_s}
36	available for retrieval.
37	Most organizations require exactly one {rbtgi_depot}.
38	
39	{at_recipe_bottle}
40	provides bash command-line administration of {rbtgi_depot} lifecycle and operations across roles.
41	
42	* *{rbtr_payor}*: Creates and funds {rbtgi_depot_s}; creates {rbtr_governor_s}. Billing flows through the {rbtr_payor_p} linked account. Manual OAuth setup enables all subsequent automation.
43	* *{rbtr_governor}*: Administers credentials within a {rbtgi_depot}. Creates {rbtr_director_s} and {rbtr_retriever_s} via service account authentication.
44	* *{rbtr_director}*: Submits builds, manages {rbtgi_image_s}.
45	* *{rbtr_retriever}*: Pulls {rbtgi_image_s} for local use.
46	
47	The
48	{rbtr_payor}
49	stands apart--requires manual console work and OAuth authentication for both {rbtgi_depot} and {rbtr_governor} lifecycle operations.
50	The
51	{rbtr_governor}
52	and all downstream roles authenticate via
53	{rbtgi_rbra_file}
54	credentials, enabling automation without human interaction.
55	
56	== Phase 1: Establish
57	
58	{rbtgo_payor_establish}
59	is a guide for manual steps taken using the Google Cloud Console to create the
60	{rbtgi_payor_project},
61	link a billing account,
62	enable required APIs,
63	and configure an OAuth 2.0 client.
64	
65	This phase produces a
66	{rbtgi_json_key_file}
67	containing OAuth client credentials.
68	This file must not be committed to version control.
69	
70	== Phase 2: Setup
71	
72	The administrator who completed establishment now executes automation from their
73	{at_workstation}.
74	
75	{rbtgo_payor_install}
76	takes the
77	{rbtgi_json_key_file}
78	and completes the OAuth handshake.
79	A browser window opens for authorization.
80	Upon success, the
81	{rbro_refresh_token}
82	is stored in `~/.rbw/rbro.env` with `600` permissions.
83	This file enables the
84	{rbtr_payor}
85	to authenticate for all future operations without repeating the browser flow.
86	
87	{rbtgo_depot_levy}
88	provisions the container build infrastructure.
89	Most installations require exactly one
90	{rbtgi_depot}-
91	a cohesive unit comprising a dedicated
92	{gcp_project_id},
93	a
94	{rbtgi_build_bucket},
95	a
96	{rbtgi_repository},
97	and the
98	{rbtr_mason}
99	service account that executes builds within the cloud.
100	
101	The
102	{rbtr_payor}
103	can manage multiple {rbtgi_depot_s}:
104	{rbtgo_depot_list}
105	displays all active {rbtgi_depot_s} and their status;
106	{rbtgo_depot_unmake}
107	permanently removes a {rbtgi_depot} and all its resources.
108	
109	The
110	{rbtr_payor}
111	then executes
112	{rbtgo_governor_mantle}
113	to create the
114	{rbtr_governor}
115	service account within the
116	{rbtgi_depot}.
117	This {rbtr_payor} operation produces an
118	{rbtgi_rbra_file},
119	stored at the path specified by
120	{rbrr_governor_rbra_file}.
121	The
122	{rbtr_governor}
123	can now operate independently of the
124	{rbtr_payor}.
125	
126	== Credential Administration
127	
128	The
129	{rbtr_governor}
130	manages the roster of credentialed users throughout the
131	{rbtgi_depot}
132	lifecycle.
133	While credential creation is typically infrequent, this administrative function remains active.
134	
135	{rbtgo_director_knight}
136	provisions a
137	{rbtr_director}
138	service account, producing an
139	{rbtgi_rbra_file}
140	at the path specified by
141	{rbrr_director_rbra_file}.
142	Directors submit builds and manage images.
143	
144	{rbtgo_retriever_charter}
145	provisions a
146	{rbtr_retriever}
147	service account, producing an
148	{rbtgi_rbra_file}
149	at the path specified by
150	{rbrr_retriever_rbra_file}.
151	Retrievers pull images for local use.
152	
153	The
154	{rbtr_governor}
155	also maintains visibility into issued credentials through
156	{rbtgo_sa_list}
157	and can revoke access through
158	{rbtgo_sa_forfeit}.
159	
160	== Credential Files
161	
162	Each {rbtr_role} maintains its own credential file.
163	Understanding these files helps administrators plan secure handling.
164	
165	The
166	{rbtr_payor}
167	credentials reside in `~/.rbw/rbro.env`, containing the
168	{rbro_client_secret}
169	and
170	{rbro_refresh_token}.
171	This file exists only on the administrator's
172	{at_workstation}.
173	
174	The
175	{rbtr_governor},
176	{rbtr_director},
177	and
178	{rbtr_retriever}
179	each have an
180	{rbtgi_rbra_file}
181	at paths defined in the repository configuration.
182	Each file contains
183	{giam_service_account}
184	credentials scoped to one role within one
185	{rbtgi_depot}.
186	
187	All credential files require `600` permissions, exclusion from version control, and secure distribution to authorized users.
188	A credential file cannot operate outside its designated role and {rbtgi_depot}.
189	
190	== Day-to-Day Operations
191	
192	Once setup and credential administration are complete, ongoing work proceeds through
193	{rbtr_director}
194	and
195	{rbtr_retriever}
196	roles.
197	
198	=== Director Operations
199	
200	The
201	{rbtr_director}
202	manages the build lifecycle.
203	
204	{rbtgo_ark_conjure}
205	submits a container build to
206	{gcb_service}
207	via `builds.create`.
208	The
209	{rbtr_mason}
210	executes the build within the cloud and publishes the resulting
211	{rbtgi_image}
212	to the
213	{rbtgi_repository}.
214	
215	{rbtgo_image_delete}
216	removes an
217	{rbtgi_image}
218	from the
219	{rbtgi_repository}.
220	
221	**MISSING**: {rbtgi_image} listing operation is not yet implemented.
222	
223	=== Retriever Operations
224	
225	The
226	{rbtr_retriever}
227	accesses built artifacts.
228	
229	{rbtgo_image_retrieve}
230	pulls an
231	{rbtgi_image}
232	from the
233	{rbtgi_repository}
234	to the local
235	{at_workstation}.
236	
237	{rbtgo_ark_inspect}
238	displays the trust posture of a locally-present
239	{rbtga_ark},
240	reading build provenance, SBOM, and vouch results from the `-about` and `-vouch` artifacts retrieved by
241	{rbtgo_ark_summon}.
242	Use {rbtc_plumb_compact} for a summary or {rbtc_plumb_full} for complete detail including package inventory and Dockerfile.
243	
244	== Recovery
245	
246	If `~/.rbw/rbro.env` is lost, the administrator obtains a fresh
247	{rbtgi_json_key_file}
248	from Google Cloud Console and re-executes
249	{rbtgo_payor_install}.
250	
251	If OAuth tokens expire,
252	{rbtgo_payor_refresh}
253	obtains fresh credentials.
254	
255	If
256	{rbtr_governor}
257	credentials are compromised or need rotation, the
258	{rbtr_payor}
259	executes
260	{rbtgo_governor_mantle}
261	to replace the existing
262	{rbtr_governor}
263	service account with a fresh one, generating new
264	{rbtgi_rbra_file}
265	credentials.
266	
267	To revoke a user's access, the
268	{rbtr_governor}
269	executes
270	{rbtgo_sa_forfeit}
271	for the target service account, invalidating the corresponding
272	{rbtgi_rbra_file}.
273

{rbtgi_depot}.

All credential files require `600` permissions, exclusion from version control, and secure distribution to authorized users.
A credential file cannot operate outside its designated role and {rbtgi_depot}.

== Day-to-Day Operations

Once setup and credential administration are complete, ongoing work proceeds through
{rbtr_director}
and
{rbtr_retriever}
roles.

=== Director Operations

The
{rbtr_director}
manages the build lifecycle.

{rbtgo_ark_conjure}
submits a container build to
{gcb_service}
via `builds.create`.
The
{rbtr_mason}
executes the build within the cloud and publishes the resulting
{rbtgi_image}
to the
{rbtgi_repository}.

{rbtgo_image_delete}
removes an
{rbtgi_image}
from the
{rbtgi_repository}.

**MISSING**: {rbtgi_image} listing operation is not yet implemented.

=== Retriever Operations

The
{rbtr_retriever}
accesses built artifacts.

{rbtgo_image_retrieve}
pulls an
{rbtgi_image}
from the
{rbtgi_repository}
to the local
{at_workstation}.

{rbtgo_ark_inspect}
displays the trust posture of a locally-present
{rbtga_ark},
reading build provenance, SBOM, and vouch results from the `-about` and `-vouch` artifacts retrieved by
{rbtgo_ark_summon}.
Use {rbtc_plumb_compact} for a summary or {rbtc_plumb_full} for complete detail including package inventory and Dockerfile.

== Recovery

If `~/.rbw/rbro.env` is lost, the administrator obtains a fresh
{rbtgi_json_key_file}
from Google Cloud Console and re-executes
{rbtgo_payor_install}.

If OAuth tokens expire,
{rbtgo_payor_refresh}
obtains fresh credentials.

If
{rbtr_governor}
credentials are compromised or need rotation, the
{rbtr_payor}
executes
{rbtgo_governor_mantle}
to replace the existing
{rbtr_governor}
service account with a fresh one, generating new
{rbtgi_rbra_file}
credentials.

To revoke a user's access, the
{rbtr_governor}
executes
{rbtgo_sa_forfeit}
for the target service account, invalidating the corresponding
{rbtgi_rbra_file}.

238	| Director | `RBRR_SECRETS_DIR/director/rbra.env` | `tt/rbw-aK.GovernorKnightsDirector.sh` |
239	| Retriever | `RBRR_SECRETS_DIR/retriever/rbra.env` | `tt/rbw-aC.GovernorChartersRetriever.sh` |

245	Recipe Bottle uses a Config Regime system — structured configuration with typed validation. Each regime has a render command (display current values) and a validate command (check correctness).
246

248	
249	| Regime | File | Purpose | Render | Validate |
250	|--------|------|---------|--------|----------|
251	| RBRP | `.rbk/rbrp.env` | Payor GCP project identity | `tt/rbw-rpr.*` | `tt/rbw-rpv.*` |
252	| RBRR | `.rbk/rbrr.env` | Depot project, region, build config | `tt/rbw-rrr.*` | `tt/rbw-rrv.*` |
253	| RBRN | `.rbk/*/rbrn.env` | Per-vessel: runtime, hallmarks | `tt/rbw-rnr.*` | `tt/rbw-rnv.*` |
254	| RBRV | vessel dirs | Container image build definitions | `tt/rbw-rvr.*` | `tt/rbw-rvv.*` |

258	
259	| Regime | Purpose | Generated by |
260	|--------|---------|-------------|
261	| RBRO | OAuth refresh token | `tt/rbw-gPI.PayorInstall.sh` |
262	| RBRA | Service account credentials | `tt/rbw-aM.*` / `tt/rbw-aK.*` / `tt/rbw-aC.*` |
263	
264	### BUK Base Regimes
265	
266	| Regime | File | Purpose |
267	|--------|------|---------|
268	| BURC | `.buk/burc.env` | Project structure (tabtarget dir, tools dir) |
269	| BURS | `../station-files/burs.env` | Developer machine (log dir). Not in git. |
270	
271	## Vessels and Nameplates
272	
273	**Vessels** are build definitions — each is a directory under `rbev-vessels/`:
274	
275	```
276	rbev-vessels/
277	├── rbev-sentry-debian-slim/    # Sentry vessel (conjure — built from source)
278	│   ├── Dockerfile
279	│   └── rbrv.env
280	├── rbev-bottle-ifrit/          # Ifrit bottle vessel (conjure — built from source)
281	│   ├── Dockerfile
282	│   └── rbrv.env
283	└── rbev-bottle-plantuml/       # PlantUML server (bind — upstream image pinned by digest)
284	    └── rbrv.env
285	```
286	
287	Conjure vessels have a Dockerfile and are built by Cloud Build. Bind vessels (like `rbev-bottle-plantuml`) pin an external image by digest in `rbrv.env` — no Dockerfile, no build step. Graft vessels push a locally-built image to GAR via docker push — no Cloud Build for the image, but about and vouch still run in Cloud Build. The same `tt/rbw-hO.DirectorOrdainsHallmark.sh` command handles all three: it detects the vessel mode and triggers a Cloud Build (conjure), mirrors from upstream (bind), or pushes a local image (graft). 290	
291	```
292	.rbk/tadmor/rbrn.env          # Maps tadmor → rbev-sentry-debian-slim + rbev-bottle-ifrit

295	So `tt/rbw-cC.Charge.tadmor.sh` charges the crucible defined by the `tadmor` nameplate, which selects its sentry and bottle vessel images.

## Setup

Recipe Bottle uses a role-based security model with four roles:

| Role | Authenticates via | Purpose |
|------|-------------------|---------|
| [**Payor**](#Payor) | OAuth (browser flow) | Creates/funds GCP infrastructure, manages [governor](#Governor) lifecycle |
| [**Governor**](#Governor) | Service account credential | Administers [director](#Director) and [retriever](#Retriever) credentials within a [depot](#Depot) |
| [**Director**](#Director) | Service account credential | Submits builds, manages images, verifies provenance |
| [**Retriever**](#Retriever) | Service account credential | Pulls images for local use |

The payor stands apart — it requires manual Google Cloud Console work and OAuth authentication. All downstream roles authenticate via credential files, enabling full automation.

### Onboarding Guide

The onboarding guide detects which roles you have credentials for and routes you to the right walkthrough:

```
tt/rbw-go.OnboardMAIN.sh
```

Each role has its own walkthrough that probes your progress and shows the next step. For a full health dashboard across all roles:

```
tt/rbw-gOr.OnboardReference.sh
```

### Phase 1: Payor Establishment

This phase involves manual work in the Google Cloud Console: creating a GCP project, enabling APIs, and configuring an OAuth consent screen. The guided procedure walks you through each click.

1. **Configure payor project** — Edit `.rbk/rbrp.env` and set `RBRP_PAYOR_PROJECT_ID` to your desired GCP project ID (must be globally unique across all of GCP, 6-30 characters, lowercase letters/numbers/hyphens).

2. **Establish payor** — Follow the guided procedure to create the GCP project and configure OAuth consent screen:
   ```
   tt/rbw-gPE.PayorEstablish.sh
   ```
   This displays step-by-step instructions for the Google Cloud Console. At the end you will download a JSON key file containing OAuth client credentials.

3. **Install OAuth credentials** — Ingest the JSON key file downloaded during establishment. A browser window will open for authorization:
   ```
   tt/rbw-gPI.PayorInstall.sh ~/Downloads/client_secret_*.json
   ```
   On success, the refresh token is stored in `~/.rbw/rbro.env` with `600` permissions. You will not need to repeat the browser flow.

### Phase 2: Infrastructure Provisioning

5. **Review depot configuration** — Edit `.rbk/rbrr.env` to set region, machine type, and other depot parameters. Use the render command to review:
   ```
   tt/rbw-rrr.RenderRepoRegime.sh
   ```

6. **Levy depot** — This provisions the GCP depot project with build infrastructure, artifact registry, and secrets. This binds your RBRR configuration to real cloud resources:
   ```
   tt/rbw-dL.PayorLeviesDepot.sh <depot-name>
   ```

7. **Mantle governor** — Admin service account for the depot:
   ```
   tt/rbw-aM.PayorMantlesGovernor.sh
   ```

### Phase 3: Credential Creation (Governor role)

8. **Knight director** — Build service account. The instance name labels this director — use a short identifier:
   ```
   tt/rbw-aK.GovernorKnightsDirector.sh <instance-name>
   ```

9. **Charter retriever** — Image pull service account. Use the same instance name as your director:
   ```
   tt/rbw-aC.GovernorChartersRetriever.sh <instance-name>
   ```

### Phase 4: Build & Retrieve (Director + Retriever roles)

10. **Ordain hallmark** — Build (conjure) or mirror (bind) each vessel's image (typically 10-20 minutes for conjure builds):
    ```
    tt/rbw-hO.DirectorOrdainsHallmark.sh rbev-vessels/<vessel-name>
    ```

13. **Check & vouch** — Verify builds completed and SLSA provenance:
    ```
    tt/rbw-ht.DirectorTalliesHallmarks.sh
    tt/rbw-hV.DirectorVouchesHallmarks.sh
    ```

14. **Record hallmarks** — Copy the hallmark values from the check output into your nameplate regime file:
    ```
    # Edit .rbk/rbrn_<vessel>.env and set:
    RBRN_SENTRY_HALLMARK=c260101120000-r260101130000
    RBRN_BOTTLE_HALLMARK=c260101120000-r260101140000
    ```

15. **Summon** — Pull vouched images locally (Retriever role):
    ```
    tt/rbw-hs.RetrieverSummonsHallmark.sh <vessel-name> <hallmark>
    ```

## Day-to-Day Operations

The examples below use `tadmor` (the included test nameplate). Replace with your own nameplate moniker — imprints in tabtarget filenames match the nameplate name from `.rbk/*/rbrn.env`.

### Starting a Bottle

```
tt/rbw-cC.Charge.tadmor.sh
```

This charges the crucible — starts the sentry, pentacle, and bottle containers for the vessel.

### Diagnostics

```
tt/rbw-cr.Rack.sh tadmor    # Shell into bottle (rack the demon)
tt/rbw-ch.Hail.sh tadmor    # Shell into sentry (hail the guard)
tt/rbw-cs.Scry.sh tadmor    # Observe network traffic (scry the topology)
```

### Stopping

```
tt/rbw-cQ.Quench.tadmor.sh
```

### Plumbing Provenance

```
tt/rbw-hpf.RetrieverPlumbsFull.sh   # Full: SBOM, build info, Dockerfile
tt/rbw-hpc.RetrieverPlumbsCompact.sh # Compact summary
```

## Credential Safety

All credential files require `600` permissions and must never be committed to version control.

| Credential | Location | Created by |
|------------|----------|------------|
| Payor OAuth | `~/.rbw/rbro.env` | `tt/rbw-gPI.PayorInstall.sh` |
| Governor | `RBRR_SECRETS_DIR/governor/rbra.env` | `tt/rbw-aM.PayorMantlesGovernor.sh` |
| Director | `RBRR_SECRETS_DIR/director/rbra.env` | `tt/rbw-aK.GovernorKnightsDirector.sh` |
| Retriever | `RBRR_SECRETS_DIR/retriever/rbra.env` | `tt/rbw-aC.GovernorChartersRetriever.sh` |

Each credential file is scoped to one role within one depot and cannot operate outside its designation.

## Configuration

Recipe Bottle uses a Config Regime system — structured configuration with typed validation. Each regime has a render command (display current values) and a validate command (check correctness).

### User-Configured Regimes

| Regime | File | Purpose | Render | Validate |
|--------|------|---------|--------|----------|
| RBRP | `.rbk/rbrp.env` | Payor GCP project identity | `tt/rbw-rpr.*` | `tt/rbw-rpv.*` |
| RBRR | `.rbk/rbrr.env` | Depot project, region, build config | `tt/rbw-rrr.*` | `tt/rbw-rrv.*` |
| RBRN | `.rbk/*/rbrn.env` | Per-vessel: runtime, hallmarks | `tt/rbw-rnr.*` | `tt/rbw-rnv.*` |
| RBRV | vessel dirs | Container image build definitions | `tt/rbw-rvr.*` | `tt/rbw-rvv.*` |
| RBRS | station file | Developer machine paths (not in git) | `tt/rbw-rsr.*` | `tt/rbw-rsv.*` |

### Managed Regimes (generated by commands)

| Regime | Purpose | Generated by |
|--------|---------|-------------|
| RBRO | OAuth refresh token | `tt/rbw-gPI.PayorInstall.sh` |
| RBRA | Service account credentials | `tt/rbw-aM.*` / `tt/rbw-aK.*` / `tt/rbw-aC.*` |

### BUK Base Regimes

| Regime | File | Purpose |
|--------|------|---------|
| BURC | `.buk/burc.env` | Project structure (tabtarget dir, tools dir) |
| BURS | `../station-files/burs.env` | Developer machine (log dir). Not in git. |

## Vessels and Nameplates

**Vessels** are build definitions — each is a directory under `rbev-vessels/`:

```
rbev-vessels/
├── rbev-sentry-debian-slim/    # Sentry vessel (conjure — built from source)
│   ├── Dockerfile
│   └── rbrv.env
├── rbev-bottle-ifrit/          # Ifrit bottle vessel (conjure — built from source)
│   ├── Dockerfile
│   └── rbrv.env
└── rbev-bottle-plantuml/       # PlantUML server (bind — upstream image pinned by digest)
    └── rbrv.env
```

Conjure vessels have a Dockerfile and are built by Cloud Build. Bind vessels (like `rbev-bottle-plantuml`) pin an external image by digest in `rbrv.env` — no Dockerfile, no build step. Graft vessels push a locally-built image to GAR via docker push — no Cloud Build for the image, but about and vouch still run in Cloud Build. The same `tt/rbw-hO.DirectorOrdainsHallmark.sh` command handles all three: it detects the vessel mode and triggers a Cloud Build (conjure), mirrors from upstream (bind), or pushes a local image (graft). Trust hierarchy: conjure has full SLSA provenance, bind has digest-pin verification, graft has no provenance chain (GRAFTED verdict).

**Nameplates** tie vessels together into a runnable bottle. The nameplate moniker (e.g. `tadmor`) is what appears as the imprint in tabtarget filenames:

```
.rbk/tadmor/rbrn.env          # Maps tadmor → rbev-sentry-debian-slim + rbev-bottle-ifrit
```

So `tt/rbw-cC.Charge.tadmor.sh` charges the crucible defined by the `tadmor` nameplate, which selects its sentry and bottle vessel images.

## Testing

// Category declarations:
// rbtr_ prefix: RB Term Role (Recipe Bottle role identities)
// rbtgi_ prefix: RB Term Google Instance (Recipe Bottle specific instances)

// gar_ prefix: Google Artifact Registry (generic service)

content.text:
Found what I needed. Here's the picture, and it changes my thinking a bit.

## The two surfaces serve very different audiences

`RBSGS-GettingStarted.adoc` (272 lines) and README.md's Setup section (≈170 lines) cover the same ground but in completely different registers:

**RBSGS — definitional and abstract.** It uses MCM attribute references like `{rbtr_payor}`, `{rbtgi_depot}`, `{rbtgo_payor_establish}` that resolve through RBS0's mapping section to formal terms. The prose talks *about* what each role does, in spec voice. Look at lines 56-69:

```
== Phase 1: Establish

{rbtgo_payor_establish}
is a guide for manual steps taken using the Google Cloud Console to create the
{rbtgi_payor_project},
link a billing account, ...
```

After MCM resolution this becomes "*The payor_establish operation is a guide…*" — readable but still spec-shaped.

**README Setup — concrete and command-driven.** Same content, different shape — line 132:

```markdown
2. **Establish payor** — Follow the guided procedure to create the GCP project and configure OAuth consent screen:
   ```
   tt/rbw-gPE.PayorEstablish.sh
   ```
   This displays step-by-step instructions for the Google Cloud Console...
```

A reader sees the literal tabtarget filename, the literal command to run, and a 1-sentence "what happens next." No MCM round-trip, no abstract operation references. It's a recipe.

**These are not the same document with different formatting.** They are different documents serving different readers — one for someone who wants to understand the system, one for someone who wants to install it. The "sync" between them isn't a translation — it's parallel maintenance of two views over the same underlying truth.

## Concrete drift already exists

`RBSGS:221` literally contains: `**MISSING**: {rbtgi_image} listing operation is not yet implemented.` But README.md has tally operations and `tt/rbw-ht.DirectorTalliesHallmarks.sh` covered as part of the Build & Retrieve phase. The README has moved on; RBSGS hasn't caught up.

This is one drift point I noticed in 270 lines. There are almost certainly more.

## What this means for the slash command

A "sync RBS0 → README" command in the strong sense — reshape RBSGS into README Setup automatically — would have to:
1. Resolve all MCM attribute references against the mapping section
2. Map abstract operation references (`{rbtgo_payor_install}`) to concrete tabtarget filenames (`tt/rbw-gPI.PayorInstall.sh`)
3. Convert spec voice into recipe voice ("X provisions Y" → "Run X to do Y")
4. Reshape paragraph blocks into numbered steps with code fences
5. Decide which subset of RBS0 terms to expose to the consumer (the full mapping section is much larger than README's ~26-term glossary)
6. Preserve the existing README anchors and cross-links

That's editorial work, not transformation. An LLM can do it, but each section needs human review — and the audience-shape difference means a "sync" that mechanically copied text would be *worse* than what's in README.md today.

## Reframe — what the slash command should actually be

Given what I'm seeing, I no longer think the right shape is "/rbk-sync-readme that pulls from RBS0." That framing assumes RBS0 is the document being translated. It's not — it's the **source of truth for the term mappings and definitions**, but it's a different document than what consumers need.

The correct framing is: **README.md is a separate authored work that must stay consistent with RBS0's term definitions and operation references.** The slash command's job isn't to generate README from RBSGS — it's to verify that everywhere README talks about a Recipe Bottle term, the term's meaning matches RBS0's canonical definition.

That's a much narrower problem. The command becomes:

> `/rbk-audit-readme` — Walk README.md, identify every reference to a Recipe Bottle term (vessel, depot, payor, etc.). For each, look up the canonical definition in RBS0's mapping section. Flag drift: terms used in README that don't exist in RBS0; terms in RBS0 with definitions that contradict README's glossary; tabtarget references in README that point at non-existent colophons; operation references whose RBS0 description has diverged from README's wording. Report findings; do not auto-fix.

Audit-only is the MVP, because:
- **It's safe.** No auto-edits, no risk of overwriting human authorial decisions
- **It surfaces the problem you actually have** — drift, not missing translation
- **It's simpler to build.** Read-only, deterministic-ish, no judgment about consumer voice
- **It scales.** Run it before each release. Run it after editing RBS0. Run it whenever you suspect drift

A future `/rbk-sync-readme` that proposes fixes can be built on top of the audit, once the audit is shown to be useful. But the audit alone is enough for MVP.

## The cosmology page is a separate concern

RBSCO (301 lines) is the project narrative — vision, history, roadmap. It was the source for the current crufty `index.html`. It's a *third* document, not a glossary or a tutorial. README.md has very little of this content (it's mostly Setup + glossary + day-to-day operations). RBSCO's content is closer to a "what is Recipe Bottle and why does it exist" page.

**Question for you**: does any RBSCO content need to reach customers at all, or is it internal context for prospective contributors / your future self? If it's the latter, RBSCO stays in `vov_veiled/` forever and never ships, and the consumer surface is just README. If it's the former, you'll want to fold a small chunk of it into README's intro section, and the cosmology page concern dissolves.

## Revised MVP

Bringing it together — three things, in order:

1. **`/rbk-audit-readme` slash command** — read-only drift detector against RBS0's mapping section. Smallest useful version: enumerate README terms in `<a id="...">` tags, look each up in RBS0, report mismatches. Can grow to check tabtarget references, operation descriptions, etc.

2. **`tt/rbw-gh.RenderHelp.sh` (or similar) — pandoc-driven local render of README.md → `index.html`** with dark theme CSS and inline SVG substitution. Output to scratch path. This is what you'd preview locally and what would eventually publish. No publishing yet.

3. **One-time fold of RBSCO highlights into README** — if any cosmology content needs to ship to customers, port it as a short README intro section now (maybe 20-30 lines), and retire the cosmology-as-separate-page concept. If nothing needs to ship, RBSCO stays internal and we move on.

After these three: the loop is "edit README → audit → render → preview." All local, no publishing. Versioning, github.io subdirectories, RBRR variable, prep-release integration — all explicitly deferred until this loop has been used in anger and proven.

## Three questions for you

1. **Audit-only as MVP, agreed?** Or do you actively want the slash command to propose fixes from day one? (My read: audit-only is the right starting point. Proposed fixes can come later.)

2. **Does any RBSCO content need to ship to customers?** If no, the cosmology page concern dissolves and we focus only on README. If yes, even a small subset, we need to decide what.

3. **What's the right name for the slash command and the render tabtarget?** Names matter — I'd want them to fit cleanly into the existing prefix discipline. `/rbk-audit-readme` and `tt/rbw-gh.RenderHelp.sh` are placeholders. Worth minting properly before any code lands.

Want me to also peek at how RBSCO and README's intro overlap, to answer question 2 with data?

message.content: There is one critical other driver in this process: Tools/rbk/rbho_onboarding.sh is my intended means of helping people in various roles get to know the project.  It is in fact a primary function of all of this: the links rendered by the onboarding process need to land somewhere, and we are working that out now.

2ada3b66 jjb:1011-95dc8bec:₢A3AAJ:n: Retire rbgm_ManualProcedures.sh; split into rbho_onboarding.sh (handbook cross-role walkthroughs, thin deps) and rbhp_payor.sh (handbook payor-only ceremonies, full GCP stack). Delete rbgm_onboarding() retired stub, rbgm_LEGACY_setup_admin() ITCH marker, and zrbgm_po_review_defaults (truly unused). Migrate 6 walkthrough functions + probe helpers (po_status, po_extract_capture, probe_role_credentials, probe_retriever/director/governor/payor_units, triage_role) to zrbho_*; migrate 3 payor ceremonies (rbhp_establish, rbhp_refresh, rbhp_quota_build — quota rename deferred out of scope per docket) + full zrbhp_* display combinators and ZRBHP_* color state. Slim rbhp_kindle — drop unused temp-file constants that were for retired LEGACY_setup_admin and ONBOARDING_MONIKER/_NAMEPLATE that were only referenced from retired rbgm_onboarding dead code. Split rbgm_cli.sh into rbho_cli.sh (thin: buh/buz/rbcc/rbgc/rbz) and rbhp_cli.sh (full regime+OAuth+IAM stack). Update rbz_zipper.sh colophon routing: rbw-go/rbw-gO* -> rbho_cli.sh, rbw-gPE/rbw-gPR/rbw-gq -> rbhp_cli.sh (rbw-gPI remains in rbgp_cli.sh as docket predicted). Update CLAUDE.md acronym map (remove RBGM, add RBHO + RBHP with rbh*=Handbook family note and reserved RBHG/RBHD/RBHR slots). Update RBS0-SpecTop.adoc 3 display-text refs to point at new function names. Docket deviation: the probe_*_units and po_* helpers were listed in docket as 'used only by old monolithic' but are actually called directly by per-role walkthroughs — they were migrated to rbho as zrbho_* rather than deleted. Validation: tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.fast.sh 75/75, tt/rbw-tf.QualifyFast.sh passes (tabtarget structure, colophon registrations, generated context), manual smoke rbw-go/gOR/gOD/gOG/gOP/gOr/gPE/gPR/gq all render with OSC-8 hyperlinks and resolved tabtarget colophons. Size limit raised to 150000 — rbho_onboarding.sh (~42KB) plus rbhp_payor.sh (~21KB) plus two new cli files plus full rbgm_ManualProcedures.sh deletion (1867 lines) make this unsplittable: partial commits would leave zipper routing broken or create dangling symbol references. Unblocks later A3 paces that reshape role tracks inside rbho_onboarding.sh.
0beb4e80 jjb:1011-7d303b18:₢A3AAI:n: Rename BUK guide module to handbook: bug_guide.sh -> buh_handbook.sh, sweep bug_/zbug_/ZBUG_ -> buh_/zbuh_/ZBUH_ across 8 consumer files (~900 occurrences, 704 in rbgm_ManualProcedures.sh alone), update CLAUDE.md acronym map (BUG -> BUH) and BUK README module prefix table; rename bug-link test fixture to buh-link. Header text 'Bash Utility Guide' -> 'Bash Utility Handbook' and error tag 'bug:' -> 'buh:' in renamed module. Validation: BUK self-test 18/18, rbtd fast suite 75/75, manual smoke rbw-go/rbw-gOR render correctly with OSC-8 hyperlinks and tabtarget resolution intact. Size limit raised to 150000 bytes (default 50000) because this is a pure mechanical rename — one identifier, atomic by nature, partial commits would leave the system broken.

content.text:
This reframes the whole conversation. Let me say what I see and then propose a much smaller MVP than I'd been heading toward.

## What `rbho_onboarding.sh` tells me

- **53 link calls** across the file (`buh_tlt`/`buh_tl`)
- **27 unique anchor targets** — exactly the same 27 as the README glossary, no more, no less
- The links live inside **tightly-woven pedagogical sequences**, not term lists. Each role track is a teaching script: a term gets defined in one inline sentence, then the link is offered for the user who wants to read more. From `rbho_director` line 671:

  ```bash
  buh_tlt "  Base images (" "enshrine" "${z_docs}#Enshrine" "): the upstream images that vessels build FROM."
  ```

  The user is mid-track. They've just been told what enshrining means in one sentence. They click the link if they want more depth. Whatever loads must (a) load fast, (b) put them at the *Enshrine* row, (c) not bury them in unrelated content.

## What this tells me about the landing page

The audience for the landing page is **a customer in the middle of an onboarding walkthrough, in a specific role track, who just clicked a term inline.** That audience's needs are very narrow:

| Need | Required of the landing page |
|---|---|
| Loads in <1 second | Static HTML, small payload |
| Lands at the right term | Anchor resolution must work — case-insensitively or exactly |
| Centered on term-lookup, not project tour | Page must be glossary-shaped, not narrative-shaped |
| Easy to follow related terms | Cross-links between glossary entries (which README already has) |
| Survives every release | Either auto-updated, or version-pinned, or both |

**The cosmology page (current `index.html`) fails every row except the first.** It's narrative, has no anchors, isn't centered on the user's term. README.md's glossary table satisfies every row. So the answer to "where should the links land" is: at README.md, in some renderable form. The cosmology page was solving a different problem (project tour for prospective contributors) and was never going to serve onboarding clicks well.

## The MVP collapses to three concrete actions

Now that the audience is clear, the MVP is much smaller than I'd been proposing:

1. **Push `README.md` to `scaleinv/recipebottle-staging`** as a single-file PR. GitHub renders it on the repo page automatically. The case-insensitive anchor shim (which you verified) makes the existing capitalized fragments work.

2. **Retarget `RBGC_PUBLIC_DOCS_URL` to `https://github.com/scaleinv/recipebottle-staging`.** One constant change in `Tools/rbk/rbgc_Constants.sh`. The onboarding's 53 links now resolve to README's glossary on GitHub.

3. **Verify one onboarding link end-to-end.** Run `tt/rbw-go.OnboardMAIN.sh`, click any term, confirm the browser scrolls to the right glossary row. If yes, MVP is done.

**That's it.** Everything else — pandoc, inline SVG, version pinning, RBRR variable, prep-release ceremony integration, RBS0 sync slash commands, retiring `index.html` — is *next-step work*, not MVP work. Each can be argued for on its own merits, but none is required to make the onboarding links land somewhere useful.

## Why I'd defer the rest

- **Pandoc + custom render**: The motivation is brand-clean URLs and SVG-with-links. Real value, but it's a polish layer over a working baseline. Build it once you know which page shape actually serves the onboarding audience well — and you'll only know that by watching real users click.
- **Version pinning**: The motivation is "customer running v1.2.3 sees v1.2.3 docs." Real concern, but it pre-supposes you have multiple released versions in circulation. You're explicitly not at general release yet. Pin when there's something to pin against.
- **RBS0 sync slash command**: The motivation is "RBS0 and README must not drift." Real concern, but the README already exists in current form, the drift is small today, and the audit is most useful when run *before a release* — which is months away. Build the audit when the cost of drift becomes visible.
- **Retiring `index.html` entirely**: The motivation is cleanliness. Real, but `index.html` is not in the onboarding link path under the new constant. It becomes harmless legacy. Retire it whenever; nothing depends on it once the constant is retargeted.

## The "staging" wart

The one cost of Path X is the URL: `https://github.com/scaleinv/recipebottle-staging#Payor` shows "staging" to customers. You said staging is a private fork for safe experimentation, which is honest. Two ways to handle:

- **Live with it for MVP.** The "staging" suffix is honest — you said yourself you're not ready for general release. The URL telling customers "you're early" is consistent with your stance.
- **Push README to public `scaleinv/recipebottle` instead.** The branding is cleaner but you're now publishing to the public surface for real, which contradicts "not ready for general release."

I'd live with the wart for MVP. When you're actually releasing, retarget to the public repo.

## Where this leaves the rest of our discussion

The longer-arc concerns (RBS0 ↔ README drift, per-version pinning, custom rendering with SVG links) are still real. They become interesting once the MVP is in place and you start seeing where it breaks. My recommendation is to **slate them as itches** — capture the thinking we just did so it isn't lost, but don't act on them now.

Concretely, three itches:

1. **`bug_tlt` URL version-pinning via RBRR field** — once you're cutting tagged releases, embed the release ref in `RBGC_PUBLIC_DOCS_URL` so customer binaries point at the docs that match their version. (Bigger discussion: tag/branch/SHA granularity.)

2. **RBS0 ↔ README drift audit** — once drift becomes a maintenance pain, build `/rbk-audit-readme` as a read-only checker that compares README's glossary entries against RBS0's mapping section and flags inconsistencies. Don't auto-fix; just surface drift.

3. **Pandoc-rendered `index.html` with inline SVG glossary links** — once you want richer presentation and clickable architecture-diagram-to-glossary navigation, build the render pipeline. This unlocks features GitHub default rendering can't provide.

4. *(maybe)* **`README.consumer.md` retirement** — `Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/README.consumer.md` is stale; the prep-release Step 8 still copies it onto README.md. This is the foot-gun I flagged earlier. Either retire the template or sync it to current README. Orthogonal to everything else.

## Three questions for you

1. **Are you good with the collapsed MVP?** Push README to staging, retarget the constant, verify one link. Three actions, no new tooling, no architecture commitment.

2. **Live with `recipebottle-staging` in the URL, or push to public `scaleinv/recipebottle` instead?** I lean staging for honesty.

3. **Slate the four itches now**, or wait until we've discussed each more? I think slating them now is right — they're already shaped enough that capture beats forgetting.

If yes to all three, I can produce the three concrete edits (push commands for the staging PR, the constant change, the verification step) and we can land MVP in this session.

message.content:
I find your argument to retire index.html and README.consumer.md compelling.  I don't remember what is in the "root" README.md but I'm now sure we should rewrite it to be some harmonious combo of index.html and vocabulary link landing page.  I'm actually good with pushing all the way to scaleinv/recipebottle the README.md and index.html retirement so that we can test with official links.  I want to add the RBRR root item connecting user's install to the right page- I need your help thinking this through given how future release tags will be managed.  I don't even want to try and slate paces for other steps than getting through first onboarding development (that's a different chat) and posting a valid initial imprint, though probably during our actual first release we'll want to rereview.  

Thoughts?  Lets not start yet, I need a little more chat first.

content.text:
Good — the scope is narrowing in a useful way. Let me give you the material you need to think on, no edits, no commits.

## What's actually in current README.md (since you said you don't remember)

I just read it. **348 lines, with a real shape:**

- Lines 1-22: Title + intro paragraph + a 2-line elevator pitch
- Lines 24-54: **`## Key Concepts`** — the 27-row glossary table with `<a id>` anchors and inline cross-links. This is the link landing target for onboarding.
- Lines 56-77: **`## How It Works`** — short narrative subsections (Image Management, Bottle Orchestration). Bridges the glossary to the architecture.
- Lines 78-97: **`## Prerequisites`** + **`## Using the CLI`** — installation prereqs and CLI invocation pattern.
- Lines 98-196: **`## Setup`** — the role table, onboarding guide pointer, and four phases (Payor / Infrastructure / Credentials / Build & Retrieve) with numbered steps and code fences.
- Lines 197-229: **`## Day-to-Day Operations`** — charge/quench/diagnostics/plumb.
- Lines 230-241: **`## Credential Safety`** — file paths and 600 permissions table.
- Lines 243-269: **`## Configuration`** — RBRP/RBRR/RBRN/RBRV/RBRS/BURC/BURS regime tables.
- Lines 271-296: **`## Vessels and Nameplates`** — mode breakdown.
- Lines 297-348: **`## Testing`** and a few closing sections.

So README.md today is **already 80% of the harmonious combo you want**. It has the glossary table (link landing), it has Setup phases (tutorial), it has narrative bridges. What it does *not* have is the cosmology page's content — the project vision, the security model rationale, the SLSA-L3 supply-chain story. Those bits live in `RBSCO-CosmologyIntro.adoc` and got rendered into the current `index.html`.

A "harmonious combo" rewrite is therefore:

1. **Front-port a small intro section** from RBSCO into README's top — vision/security-model/supply-chain-story in maybe 30-50 lines. This becomes the first thing a customer sees on the rendered page.
2. **Keep the existing glossary table** as-is (it's already the right shape and has all 27 anchors).
3. **Keep Setup, Day-to-Day, Configuration, etc.** as-is.
4. **Maybe add a small "Architecture" subsection** that references the SVG diagram (even without inline-SVG-with-links for MVP, the picture is still useful).

This is a *port-and-adjust* job, not a *write-from-scratch* job. The current README doesn't need to be thrown out — it needs an introductory chapter.

## The RBRR field — design space, with the version-management question front and center

You're right that the design depends on how release tags will be managed. Let me lay out the trade space cleanly, because the variable's shape is downstream of the tag convention.

### The fundamental question: how mutable are docs after release?

This is the hinge. Three positions:

**Position 1 — Docs are immutable per release.** Once `v1.2.3` ships, its README is frozen. Found a typo? Customer running v1.2.3 sees the typo until they upgrade to v1.2.4. The binary version and the doc version move together, always.
- *Consequence*: RBRR holds an immutable git tag (`v1.2.3`)
- *Pros*: Maximum traceability. Reproducible. Customer always sees exactly what shipped with their binary.
- *Cons*: Doc fixes can't reach existing customers. Every typo costs a version bump.

**Position 2 — Docs are mutable within a release line.** `v1.2.x` has its own release branch. Doc-only fixes land on the branch and reach existing customers next time they click a link. New features still require version bumps.
- *Consequence*: RBRR holds a release branch name (`release/v1.2`)
- *Pros*: Can fix docs without bumping versions. Customer running v1.2.3 sees corrected v1.2.x docs.
- *Cons*: Docs at a given URL may change over time. Less reproducible. Harder to answer "what did v1.2.3 ship with exactly."

**Position 3 — Docs always reflect main.** RBRR holds `main`. Every customer always sees latest. Drift between binary and docs is inevitable but accepted.
- *Consequence*: RBRR holds the literal string `main`
- *Pros*: Trivially simple. No release ceremony for docs. Iteration is instant.
- *Cons*: Customer running v1.2.3 may see docs describing v1.5.0 features. The whole point of version-pinning is given up.

These are real positions. Pick one or one becomes hybridized.

### My read of which fits you

You said earlier "iterate on onboarding and help materials." That phrase is incompatible with **Position 1** — Position 1 forces every doc tweak through a version bump and customers don't see the tweak until they upgrade. If you want iteration, you need Position 2 or Position 3.

You also said you want customers to be able to "track back to a version in github." Position 3 doesn't do this — there's no version dimension. You need Position 1 or Position 2.

**The intersection is Position 2: release branches.** Each release line has a long-lived branch; binaries point at the branch; doc fixes land on the branch and propagate.

If Position 2 fits, then the field shape is straightforward:

```bash
# In rbrr.env (regime instance — varies per release)
RBRR_PUBLIC_DOCS_REF="release/v0.1"   # release branch in public repo
```

For in-source-tree development before any release exists, the value defaults to `main`:

```bash
RBRR_PUBLIC_DOCS_REF="main"
```

And the URL composition lives in code as a join of a stable base + the regime ref:

```bash
# Tools/rbk/rbgc_Constants.sh
readonly RBGC_PUBLIC_DOCS_BASE="https://github.com/scaleinv/recipebottle/blob"
readonly RBGC_PUBLIC_DOCS_README="README.md"
# Composed where bug_tlt callers actually use it:
readonly RBGC_PUBLIC_DOCS_URL="${RBGC_PUBLIC_DOCS_BASE}/${RBRR_PUBLIC_DOCS_REF}/${RBGC_PUBLIC_DOCS_README}"
```

The split between RBGC (constants — base URL, filename) and RBRR (per-release — the ref segment) is load-bearing: the structure is locked, only the ref varies.

### The "valid initial imprint" question

You said you want to "post a valid initial imprint." I read that as: ship the first real version of the docs to public, even though you're not cutting a binary release yet. Like the first plate of a book run.

For Position 2, this means:
- Decide a release branch name *now* (`release/v0.0`? `release/preview-1`? `main` itself?)
- Push README.md to that branch on `scaleinv/recipebottle`
- Set RBRR's default to that branch name
- The first imprint is "we have content at a stable git ref that customer binaries can point at"

The simplest first imprint is to **just use `main`** in the public repo. Push README to public main. Set `RBRR_PUBLIC_DOCS_REF="main"`. When you cut your first actual release later, you create a `release/v0.1` branch from main and update RBRR. Until then, "the imprint" is "the content of main." This defers the branching convention without preventing it.

That's the lightest-weight first imprint that's still on the path to Position 2.

### Open questions only you can answer

1. **Position 1, 2, or 3?** I lean Position 2 based on what you've said you want, but it's your call.
2. **First imprint = main, or first imprint = a branch from day one?** I lean `main` for simplicity (defer the branching), but if you want to mint the convention now, that's also defensible.
3. **Where does prep-release fit?** Today, prep-release pushes to the staging fork. After this work, it'll need to ALSO know how to update RBRR's `RBRR_PUBLIC_DOCS_REF` value. That's a future-prep-release concern but worth mentioning so it's not a surprise later.
4. **What about index.html on the public repo?** You said retire it. That means the PR to public is `+ README.md` and `- index.html` (and probably the legacy `podman-gateway-proposal.md` too — does it still belong?). What about `LICENSE`, `.nojekyll`, `rbm-abstract-drawio.svg`? Those should stay.
5. **What does the public repo's `scaleinv.github.io/recipebottle/` URL show after the cut?** With `index.html` removed and `.nojekyll` still present, GitHub Pages 404s the root. This is fine *if* nothing references it anymore. The bug_tlt URLs will point at github.com, not github.io, so the github.io URL becomes orphan. Acceptable, but worth being explicit about — if anyone has bookmarked the github.io URL (or there are external references in old project pages, social media, etc.), they'll start getting 404s.

## Two specific things I want to flag before any code lands

**Flag 1 — `bhyslop/recipemuster` vs `scaleinv/recipebottle`.** The README I'd push is the one in this dev repo (`bhyslop/recipemuster`). I confirmed earlier that GitHub renders that README on its own repo page with correct anchor behavior. But the public repo is a *different* repo (`scaleinv/recipebottle`). The README content gets copied over by hand or by the prep-release ceremony. We need to be deliberate that **the README content authored here is what lands in the public repo's main branch, and stays in sync.** The current prep-release ceremony Step 8 copies `Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/README.consumer.md` → `README.md` — that's the wrong source. After this work, prep-release should copy the *root* `README.md` into the candidate (or — better — just preserve it, since it's already at the root and survives stripping).

This is the `README.consumer.md` retirement we already flagged. It becomes load-bearing for "make this work end-to-end."

**Flag 2 — The first push to public is special.** Pushing to `scaleinv/recipebottle` with `+ README.md`, `- index.html` is your **first commit on that repo that didn't come from the prep-release ceremony.** That sets a precedent. You probably want this commit to be authored by you on a branch, with a PR you can review on GitHub before merging. Not a direct push to main. The user's ability to review on the GitHub UI is the safety net.

## What I'd suggest discussing next

Three threads, in priority order:

1. **Pick a Position (1/2/3) for doc mutability.** This determines the RBRR shape and the tag convention.
2. **Decide the first imprint shape.** `main` only for now? Or mint a release branch from day one?
3. **Talk through the README rewrite scope.** What from RBSCO needs to fold in? Anything else you want to add or restructure beyond the cosmology port?

I'm not going to start anything until you say so. Want to pull on Position first, or on the rewrite scope first?

message.content:
I think position 2 is fine.  I'm not terribly worried about this for first release, I just want link naming to be correct so that we can fix it later. 

Indeed a PR process is needed between recipemuster to recipebottle.  We can defer this from the steps we are discussing now and conclude with a good push to recipemuster.

Lets now chat about README contents.  I generally think the index.html scope is correct though very behind in terms.  Ask clarifying questions about landing a good first README.

1	// RBSCO-CosmologyIntro.adoc
2	//
3	// Recipe Bottle Cosmology Overview — the nosebleed-terse "what this is"
4	//
5	// PURPOSE: This document is the index.html frontmatter source for the
6	// Recipe Bottle project page (scaleinv.github.io/recipebottle).
7	//
8	// ANCESTRY: Renamed from index.adoc (last committed 84323c31, 2025-08-18).
9	// Full git lineage: 31 commits from 2642121c through 84323c31.
10	//
11	// DEPENDENCY: Requires RBS0-SpecTop.adoc mapping section for attribute resolution.
12	
13	== Overview
14	
15	The
16	{at_rbm_system}
17	enables developers to safely run untrusted containers -
18	a significantly distinct use case than typical container deployments of carefully crafted code.
19	
20	While containers excel at packaging known applications,
21	running third-party or experimental code poses security risks.
22	The
23	{at_rbm_system}
24	addresses this by interposing a security layer
25	({at_sentry_container})
26	between untrusted containers
27	({at_bottle_container_s})
28	and system resources,
29	without requiring modifications to existing container images.
30	
31	This approach allows developers to leverage the vast ecosystem of containerized tools while maintaining strict security boundaries -
32	essential for small teams experimenting with diverse, potentially risky codebases.
33	
34	== Accomplished Infrastructure
35	
36	The
37	{at_rbm_system}
38	migrated from GitHub Actions and GHCR to Google Cloud Build (GCB) and Google Artifact Registry (GAR) in August 2025.
39	GHCR violated OCI specification for reference counting, and GitHub Actions' shared runner environment enabled cache poisoning attacks — both unacceptable for building containers from untrusted code.
40	
41	GCB and GAR provide isolated build environments, SLSA Build Level 3 attestations (verified end-to-end March 2026), reference-counted layer management, and native vulnerability scanning.
42	The project's build infrastructure was converted from Makefiles to pure bash, and the container runtime switched from podman to docker in December 2025.
43	
44	== The {at_rbm_system} Vision
45	
46	.RBM System Architecture
47	image::rbm-abstract-drawio.svg[RBM Architecture,600,400,align="center"]
48	
49	Maintaining build and service environments is a headache for the small development organization.
50	{st_container_s}
51	are an amazing tool for controlling package version constellations.
52	Necessary, but insufficient; I find that I need more redundancy, security, and control for my build setups.
53	
54	The
55	{at_rbm_system}
56	is my answer for filling in the gaps.
57	The vision is simple: how can an
58	{at_operator}
59	run only a few apps natively on a
60	{at_workstation}
61	to coordinate and arrange
62	{st_container_s}
63	in sophisticated yet safe ways for me and my customer's work?
64	
65	{at_rbm_system} uses only `bash`, `git`, `curl`, `openssh`, `jq` and a
66	{at_runtime}
67	(currently `docker`) natively to produce a safe and controlled space for development involving
68	{st_container_s}
69	heavily.
70	{at_rbm_system}
71	itself is largely a set of bash scripts designed to be easily incorporated into arbitrary projects via
72	`git subtree`, `git subrepo`, or `git submodule`
73	graft.
74	This is purely an open-source undertaking.
75	
76	=== Part One: {st_image} Management
77	
78	The
79	{at_rbm_system}
80	streamlines

160	prevents such illegal accesses.
161	
162	By providing these controlled yet accessible tools, the
163	{at_rbm_system}
164	enables small development teams to maintain proper container hygiene throughout their workflow - from initial building through deployment and eventual cleanup.
165	This empowers organizations to leverage the expanding ecosystem of containerized development tools without requiring specialized DevOps expertise.
166	
167	== Project Direction
168	
169	The following themes represent future directions for Recipe Bottle, drawn from the project's horizon roadmap.
170	
171	**Build pipeline security hardening.**
172	Egress lockdown, VPC Service Controls, cosign image signing, and Binary Authorization are evaluated and consciously deferred.
173	Each has a defined trigger condition — they activate when compliance requirements, threat models, or deployment targets change.
174	Full analysis lives in the Cloud Build security posture documentation.
175	
176	**IAM identity infrastructure.**
177	Service account numeric IDs for faster propagation, universal read-back verification after IAM grants, and universal SA preflight checks.
178	These reduce retry frequency and strengthen grant correctness guarantees.
179	
180	**Container runtime expansion.**
181	Rootless deployment, macOS-native workflows, and alternative container runtimes beyond the current docker-on-Linux model.

content.text:
OK, I have a clear picture of the cosmology scope now. Eight questions, grouped by what they decide.

## Voice and audience

**1. First-person or third-person?** RBSCO is in first-person ("my answer for filling in the gaps", "I find that I need more redundancy"). The current README is in third-person ("Recipe Bottle builds container images…"). The first-person voice is part of what gives RBSCO its character — it reads like a project diary entry from the author. Third-person is more neutral and consumer-facing. Pick one register and stick to it. Which feels right for the harmonious combo?

**2. Who is the imagined reader of the very first paragraph?** Three plausible answers, each shapes a different opening:

- "A developer who has never heard of Recipe Bottle and is deciding whether it's worth a closer look." → Opening leads with the *problem* (running untrusted containers safely) and the *one-line solution*.
- "A developer who clicked a glossary link from inside their onboarding session and accidentally landed at the top of the page." → Opening leads with a 1-line orientation and a "skip to glossary" pointer.
- "A reviewer / future contributor evaluating the project's seriousness." → Opening leads with the security model and SLSA-L3 bona fides.

The first-paragraph reader should be one of these, not a vague "any visitor." Which?

## Section fate

**3. Significant Events timeline — keep, prune, or retire?** This is RBSCO's most personal section, 110+ lines of dated entries from Dec 2023 through Mar 2026, including failed podman experiments, eBPF backstory, GHCR abandonment, etc. Three positions:

- **Keep verbatim.** Project legitimacy — "look how long we've been at this and what we tried." Reads as authentic history.
- **Prune to milestones.** Drop the failure stories, keep the major events. ~20 entries instead of 30+.
- **Retire entirely.** Consumer doesn't need history; this stays in `vov_veiled/` for internal reference.

I lean prune-to-milestones for a customer-facing README, but you may feel the failure stories ARE the personality and shouldn't be cut. Your call.

**4. The Vision section's "Part One / Part Two" subsections — preserve, condense, or replace?** These are 80+ lines together. They overlap heavily with what README already says under "How It Works" (Image Management + Bottle Orchestration). The cosmology version is more philosophical; the README version is more operational. Preserve both? Condense the cosmology version to a single subsection? Or let README's existing "How It Works" be the canonical version and skip the cosmology Part One/Part Two entirely?

**5. Project Direction (roadmap themes) — port, port-and-soften, or skip?** RBSCO ends with three forward-looking themes (build security, IAM, runtime expansion). Useful for managing expectations, but committing to specific themes in a consumer doc means they need maintenance. Skip is the lowest-maintenance option; port-and-soften (e.g., "Recipe Bottle is actively evolving — see the project page for current direction") avoids the maintenance burden while still gesturing at futures.

## What "behind in terms" means concretely

**6. When you say RBSCO is "very behind in terms," can you give me 2-3 examples of what's actually stale?** I want to make sure I understand the scope. Possibilities I can guess at, but you should confirm:

- **"RBM System" vs "Recipe Bottle"** — RBSCO uses `{at_rbm_system}` throughout. Has the project's name/branding consolidated to "Recipe Bottle"?
- **podman references in the main text** — RBSCO already says "currently `docker`" in the Vision. But the historical narrative still mentions podman extensively. Drop the podman backstory entirely, or keep as history?
- **Missing terms** — RBSCO predates current concepts like `hallmark`, `vouch`, `kludge`, `graft`, `bind`, the four-mode ordain pipeline, etc. None of these appear. Should the rewrite *add* mentions of these (with cross-links to the glossary), or are they too detailed for the intro?
- **Service account naming** — RBSCO mentions Governor / Director / Mason / Retriever. Current code uses Payor / Governor / Director / Retriever (Mason is internal-only as the cloud-side build SA). Need to align?

What else? The user-facing point of "behind in terms" matters for scoping the rewrite.

## Mechanics

**7. Architecture diagram inclusion?** The SVG (`rbm-abstract-drawio.svg`) is referenced by RBSCO and the rendered index.html. Should it appear in the README? Three options:

- **Yes, embedded** — `![RBM Architecture](rbm-abstract-drawio.svg)` in the README intro. GitHub renders inline. No clickable internal links, but the picture is visible.
- **Yes, with caveat** — Embed the image, with a note that interactive links are a future-rendering goal.
- **No** — Skip until the pandoc render pipeline can do inline-SVG-with-links properly. Defer the diagram until it can pull its weight.

I lean **Yes, embedded.** Even non-interactive, the picture is worth more than its absence. The interactivity is a future polish.

**8. Cross-linkification of new content?** When porting cosmology paragraphs, terms like "bottle", "sentry", "depot", "hallmark", etc. should ideally become links to the existing glossary anchors (`[bottle](#Bottle)`). RBSCO doesn't have these — its terms are MCM-attribute references that resolve to plain text. Do you want me to add `[term](#Term)` cross-links wherever appropriate during the port? It's the most labor-intensive part of the rewrite but the highest value for the link-landing audience (they can hop between definitions).

## One meta-question

**9. Is the rewrite "first draft + you edit" or "iterate together in chat"?** Two work modes:

- **First draft** — I do the full rewrite as one pass and you edit/critique afterward. Faster to a working artifact, but you'll be reacting to my style choices rather than steering them.
- **Iterate together** — We agree on structure first, then I draft section by section, you give feedback per section. Slower but the result is more under your direction.

I lean **iterate together** because you've been doing real thinking-out-loud about voice and shape, and a first-draft pass risks me committing to the wrong register. But if you want speed over collaboration, first-draft is fine.

---

Take whichever questions you want first. The most consequential for the actual content are 1, 2, 3, and 6 — those bound the scope and tone. The rest are mechanical.
